title: Regression Analysis
coreConcept: Regression analysis comprises statistical processes for estimating the relationships between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables. It establishes predictive models and quantifies the exact impact of predictors on an outcome.
theory:
## Modeling Relationships

Regression translates correlational observation into an actionable mathematical equation.

### Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Linear Regression
Models the relationship as a linear combination of features: $\mathbf{y} = X\mathbf{\beta} + \mathbf{\epsilon}$. OLS seeks the parameter vector $\beta$ that minimizes the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS). The closed-form analytical solution is:
$$ \hat{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y} $$
Assumptions of OLS (Gauss-Markov theorem) include linearity, homoscedasticity, and independence of errors.

### Logistic Regression
Despite its name, it is a classification algorithm used to model binary outcomes. It passes a linear combination of features through a sigmoid function to map predictions to probabilities:
$$ P(Y=1 | X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X)}} $$
Parameters are estimated using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) rather than closed-form OLS.

### Regularization
Techniques like Ridge ($L_2$ penalty) and Lasso ($L_1$ penalty) add constraints to the regression optimization to prevent over-reliance on any single feature, thereby mitigating overfitting.
keyTakeaways: Linear regression fits a hyperplane that minimizes the squared distances to the target variables.
The OLS closed-form solution leverages linear algebra to compute exact optimal weights.
Logistic regression models the probability of binary classes using a sigmoid transformation.
Regularization introduces mathematical penalties to enforce model simplicity and generalization.
